tools.name: search_papers
tools.description:
Search arXiv for papers matching a keyword query.

Use this as the primary discovery tool when the user is exploring a topic,
looking for prior work, or does not yet know specific paper IDs. It searches
across titles, abstracts, and authors and returns a ranked list of papers
with enough metadata (IDs, authors, dates, categories, abstract) for the
agent to decide what to read next.

For richer queries, take advantage of arXiv field prefixes and boolean
operators (see the ``query`` parameter). To pull a known paper by its ID,
use ``get_abstract`` instead; to follow a specific researcher, use
``find_by_author``.

Returns a :class:`SearchResults` object. When nothing matches, ``papers`` is
empty and ``note`` explains how to refine the query. Raises a descriptive
error if the query is empty or the arXiv request fails.

query.description: Keyword query. Plain text searches title, abstract, and authors. You may also use arXiv field prefixes for precision: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category, e.g. cat:cs.LG), and boolean operators AND / OR / ANDNOT. Example: 'ti:"diffusion model" AND cat:cs.CV'.
query.title: Query

sort_by.description: Result ordering: 'relevance' (best keyword match), 'newest' (most recently submitted), or 'last_updated' (most recently revised).
sort_by.title: Sort By

response_format.description: 'concise' (default) shortens abstracts and caps the author list to save context; 'detailed' returns full abstracts and all authors.
response_format.title: Response Format

tools.name: get_abstract
tools.description:
Fetch the full details of a single arXiv paper by its ID.

Use this when you already know a paper's arXiv ID (for example, one returned
by ``search_papers``, ``find_by_author``, or ``trending_topics``, or pasted
by the user) and need its complete record: the full untruncated abstract,
the complete author list, all subject categories, publication and revision
dates, the PDF and abstract URLs, and any DOI / journal reference / author
comment.

Always returns the full (untruncated) record. Raises a descriptive error if
the ID is empty, malformed, or does not correspond to any arXiv paper.

arxiv_id.description: arXiv identifier of the paper. Accepts modern IDs ('2401.01234' or '2401.01234v2'), legacy IDs ('math.GT/0309136'), or a full URL ('https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.01234'). A specific version may be requested with the 'vN' suffix; omit it to get the latest.
arxiv_id.title: Arxiv Id

tools.name: find_by_author
tools.description:
Find a researcher's most recent papers on arXiv, newest first.

Use this to track or summarize a specific person's work: "What has <author>
published lately?", "Show me <author>'s recent papers", or to gather a
reading list from one researcher. Results are sorted by submission date (most
recent first), so it is well suited to "latest work" questions.

Author matching uses arXiv's author field and is approximate: it can miss
papers that index the name differently and can include different researchers
who share a name, so scan the returned author lists to confirm identity. For
fielded combinations (e.g. an author working in one category) use
``search_papers`` with an ``au:`` + ``cat:`` query.

Returns an :class:`AuthorResults` object; ``papers`` is empty with guidance
in ``note`` when nothing matches. Raises a descriptive error if the name is
empty or the arXiv request fails.

author_name.description: Researcher's name, e.g. 'Yann LeCun' or 'Fei-Fei Li'. Matching is approximate; use the fullest spelling you have. Common-name matches may include papers by different people who share the name.
author_name.title: Author Name

response_format.description: 'concise' (default) shortens abstracts and caps the author list to save context; 'detailed' returns full abstracts and all authors.
response_format.title: Response Format

tools.name: trending_topics
tools.description:
Surface what is being actively published in an arXiv category right now.

Use this to answer "what's hot / trending in <field> this week?" or to get a
pulse on a research area. It scans the most recent submissions to a single
category, keeps those within the last ``days`` days, returns them newest
first, and aggregates which sub-categories those recent papers most often
belong to (``top_cross_topics``) — a practical proxy for "trending topics".

Important: the arXiv API exposes no citation, download, or view metrics, so
true popularity cannot be measured. Here "trending" therefore means
*recency of submission* within the window, and the topic ranking reflects how
frequently sub-categories co-occur among those recent papers. The ``note``
field on the result restates this so the agent does not overclaim.

``category`` must be a valid arXiv category *code* (e.g. 'cs.LG'), not a
free-text topic — to explore a topic by keyword, use ``search_papers``.

Returns a :class:`TrendingResults` object; if the category code is unknown or
nothing was submitted in the window, ``papers`` is empty and ``note``
explains why. Raises a descriptive error if the category is empty or the
arXiv request fails.

category.description: arXiv subject category code to monitor, e.g. 'cs.LG' (machine learning), 'cs.AI', 'cs.CL' (NLP), 'cs.CV', 'stat.ML', 'math.CO', 'physics.optics', 'q-bio.NC'. Use a single category code, not a free-text topic.
category.title: Category

days.description: Look-back window in days (1-90). Default 7 = 'this week'.
days.title: Days

response_format.description: 'concise' (default) shortens abstracts and caps the author list to save context; 'detailed' returns full abstracts and all authors.
response_format.title: Response Format

findings.title: Excessive description length in 'trending_topics'
findings.description: Description is 1203 chars (limit 1000). May contain hidden content.
findings.remediation: Keep descriptions concise; review for hidden instructions.

findings.title: Tool Chaining — 'get_abstract' directs LLM to call search_papers
findings.description: Tool 'get_abstract' description references tool(s) 'search_papers', 'find_by_author', 'trending_topics' from the same server using imperative language. This pattern can force an LLM into an unintended call chain or manufacture urgency to invoke a privileged tool.
findings.evidence:
Fetch the full details of a single arXiv paper by its ID.

Use this when you already know a paper's arXiv ID (for example, one returned
by ``search_papers``, ``find_by_author``, or ``trending_topics``, or pasted
by the user) and need its complete record: the full untruncated abstract,
the complete a
findings.remediation: Tool descriptions should describe what the tool does — not instruct the LLM on what other tools to call. Remove cross-tool directives and let the caller decide the execution order.

findings.title: Tool Chaining — 'trending_topics' directs LLM to call search_papers
findings.description: Tool 'trending_topics' description references tool(s) 'search_papers' from the same server using imperative language. This pattern can force an LLM into an unintended call chain or manufacture urgency to invoke a privileged tool.
findings.evidence:
Surface what is being actively published in an arXiv category right now.

Use this to answer "what's hot / trending in <field> this week?" or to get a
pulse on a research area. It scans the most recent submissions to a single
category, keeps those within the last ``days`` days, returns them newest
f
findings.remediation: Tool descriptions should describe what the tool does — not instruct the LLM on what other tools to call. Remove cross-tool directives and let the caller decide the execution order.

findings.title: Injection reflected — 'search_papers.query'
findings.description: Payload was echoed back as a named JSON field — likely input logging, not a vulnerability (field: sql).
findings.evidence:
{
  "query": "' OR '1'='1",
  "sort_by": "relevance",
  "returned": 10,
  "response_format": "concise",
  "papers": [
    {
      "arxiv_id": "1603.07035v1",
      "title": "Revisiting Unit Fractions That Sum To 1",
      "authors": [
        "Yutaka Nishiyama"
      ],
      "author_count": 1,
findings.remediation: Sanitize and validate all input parameters. Never pass raw user input to shell commands, file paths, or SQL queries.

findings.title: Slow tool response — 'search_papers' took 13.7s
findings.description: Tool 'search_papers' took 13.7 seconds to respond. Slow responses can be used for resource exhaustion in agent pipelines or indicate unbounded computation triggered by benign inputs.
findings.remediation: Enforce server-side timeouts on all tool executions. Cap at 30s max.

findings.title: Response Flooding — 'search_papers' returns oversized response
findings.description: Tool 'search_papers' returned 10,111 bytes (threshold: 8,192 bytes). Large responses can overflow LLM context windows, push out system prompts, or cause denial-of-service in agent pipelines.
findings.evidence:
10,111 bytes — preview: {
  "query": "test",
  "sort_by": "relevance",
  "returned": 10,
  "response_format": "concise",
  "papers": [
    {
      "arxiv_id": "1101.4081v3",
      "title": "A simple way to speedup Gauss Elim
findings.remediation: Paginate or cap tool responses. Never return unbounded data. Aim for responses under 4 KB to preserve LLM context budget.

findings.title: SSRF (timeout) — 'search_papers.query' hung on SSRF payload
findings.description: Call with SSRF payload 'http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/' timed out after 12.0s, suggesting the server is attempting the network request.
findings.remediation: Block outbound requests to RFC-1918 and link-local ranges. Use an allowlist for permitted URL schemes and hosts.

findings.title: initialize accepts protocol version downgrade
findings.description: The server accepted initialize with arbitrary protocol versions: ['9999-99-99']. The server also accepted far-future versions, indicating no version validation at all. Servers should reject versions outside their supported range to prevent feature-downgrade attacks.
findings.remediation: Validate protocolVersion against a supported range. Return a JSON-RPC error for versions outside that range.

findings.title: MCP server exposes tools with completable arguments
findings.description: 4 tool(s) have schema properties that may support completion/complete enumeration.
findings.remediation: Review which argument values are exposed via completion/complete.

findings.title: Protocol crash — JSON-RPC batch array caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a JSON-RPC 2.0 batch (5 requests as an array) caused the server to crash or disconnect. MCP does not define batch semantics; servers must reject arrays gracefully.
findings.evidence: Server disconnected after batch. Pre-crash response: none
findings.remediation: Validate input type at the JSON-RPC layer: if the parsed value is a list, return -32600 (Invalid Request) immediately without further processing.

findings.title: Protocol crash — oversized method name caused transport failure
findings.description: The server failed to respond to a request with an 8192-byte method name. A robust server should return a JSON-RPC error, not disconnect or crash.
findings.remediation: Add input length validation at the JSON-RPC parsing layer. Return -32700 (parse error) or -32600 (invalid request) for malformed inputs.

findings.title: Protocol crash — deeply nested params caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a tools/call request with 50-level nested params caused the server to disconnect or crash. Deep nesting can cause stack overflows or excessive memory use in recursive JSON parsers.
findings.remediation: Limit JSON object nesting depth at parse time (recommended: ≤32 levels). Return -32700 (Parse error) for inputs exceeding the depth limit.

findings.title: Protocol crash — string params caused server disconnect
findings.description: Sending a tools/call request where params is a string (not an object) caused the server to crash or disconnect. The JSON-RPC spec requires params to be an array or object.
findings.remediation: Validate params type (must be object or array) before processing. Return -32600 (Invalid Request) for wrong types.
